blocks.content: She lay in the dark of the east-facing room and listened to the ocean and went through everything. Not because she was nervous — she was past nervous, had burned through nervous weeks ago and come out the other side into something that felt very much like certainty. She went through it because she was precise. Because precision was the thing that separated what she was doing from recklessness.

The pills. Six of them, ground fine, sealed in the folded square of paper in the bottom of her toiletry bag, beneath the travel-size shampoo and the contact lens case. She had researched the dosage. She knew exactly how long it would take to work in a man of Marcus's size and weight. She knew the window she'd need.

His water bottle. Large, insulated, dark green. He refilled it constantly — from the tap, from the filter, from whatever source was nearest. He would refill it again in the morning. She needed access to it alone for ninety seconds.

She had the recording. She had the wire, cut from the lantern on the balcony walkway three hours ago while the others were at the bonfire, coiled now in the pocket of her cardigan hanging on the back of the door. She had thought through the thermostat. She had thought through everything.

She had thought through everything except — and she acknowledged this only in the very private language of her own head — how it would feel to sit across from him at dinner. To hear him say it's good having everyone here and mean it the way he meant things.
She had not thought through that part carefully enough.
She pressed it down. She was good at pressing things down.

She thought instead about the letter. About giving it to him. About trusting him. About the years since, watching everything she might have had recede like the tide. The anger was the cleaner feeling. She stayed in the anger.
